Stem: The text describes ballistic impact on multilayer graphene membranes and focuses on how a cone wave forms and propagates radially to redistribute projectile kinetic energy. Molecular dynamics simulations indicate a finite-size effect: below a critical membrane size, reflections of the cone wave from fixed boundaries can induce perforation and reduce projectile proof capability. The ballistic limit velocity is characterized by \(V_{50}\), defined operationally as the lowest impact velocity that permits penetration in simulations. An existing analytical model for an infinite thin isotropic membrane gives \(V_{50}\) in terms of \(\Gamma=m_m/m_p\), \(c_0=\sqrt{E/\rho}\), \(\varepsilon_{\max}\), and \(K_{\max}\), implying a rough scaling \(V_{50}\sim A + B/r_p\) for spherical projectiles. The proposed critical size relationship is claimed to depend only on projectile-to-membrane density ratio, membrane thickness, and projectile size.
Q1: What conditional relationships does the text claim between (i) membrane lateral size relative to a critical size and whether cone-wave reflections induce perforation, and (ii) projectile radius \(r_p\) and the scaling of \(V_{50}\) inferred from Eq. (1) under the stated assumptions?
Q2: Using only the text’s concepts, explain why the cone wave can both enhance impact resistance (energy redistribution away from the impact zone) and reduce it in finite membranes (reflection from fixed boundaries), and connect this to why a “critical membrane size” would emerge.
Q3: How would you operationally determine (a) \(V_{50}\) and (b) the “critical membrane size” in the molecular dynamics simulations described, using only criteria explicitly stated or directly implied in the text?
Q4: Propose one falsifiable simulation/experiment, grounded in the text, that tests the interpretation that cone-wave reflection drives the size effect; specify one observable outcome you would compare above vs. below the critical membrane size, and predict the qualitative trend on each side.
Q5: What exact analytical formula (full equation) does the paper give for the critical membrane size in terms of projectile size, membrane thickness, and the projectile-to-membrane density ratio?
Q6: The discussion claims the reflected-wave effect is “maximum for circular free-standing membrane geometries” and suggests future tests on polycrystalline membranes with grain boundaries. Based on the paper’s variables (membrane size, thickness, density ratio, and cone-wave reflection), what competing hypotheses could you form about how introducing grain boundaries might shift the critical membrane size, and what observations would help distinguish whether the shift is dominated by altered wave propagation/reflection versus altered failure strain/strain concentration effects?
